A dark log turns against the current where logs should drift downstream. The crocodile waits until someone steps into the shallows.
Spears rise from the reeds at a shallow ford marked with bone charms. The lizardfolk claim the crossing and demand payment in food or steel.
A clear coil of water rises from a ruined millrace and holds its shape against the current. It strikes at anyone disturbing the old channel.
The river surface looks smooth, but a pale sandbar shifts just below it. Reading the ripples reveals a safe line across.
A pile of driftwood shudders against a half-submerged stump. Crossing it sends sharp branches snapping upward around legs and packs.
A cold spring bubbles into the river through a bed of reeds. Its clean water soothes heat, bruises, and the sour taste of river mud.
A sealed bottle knocks against reeds beside a slow eddy. The note inside is water-stained but still asks for help from upstream.
A ferry rope has snapped and the boat is wedged against rocks downstream. Repairing the crossing before dark could save stranded travelers.
A small shrine bell rings under the river whenever clouds cover the sun. Villagers want it recovered before the next flood.
Bright watercress grows in a cold side channel, cleanest where the current is strongest. The roots cling hard to slick stones.
Brown reedmace heads stand above the marshy bank. Harvesting them before they burst provides food and useful fiber.
Wild mint scents a shaded bank where the soil stays damp. Cutting sprigs without uprooting the patch leaves it healthy.
Trout hold in a clear eddy behind a boulder. A patient cast lands them before the current carries the line into snags.
A muddy undercut bank hides whiskered catfish. Reaching them means bracing against the current without collapsing the bank.
Flat stones in the shallows hide crayfish and quick little claws. A basket placed downstream catches the ones that bolt.
Heavy gravel settles in the inside bend after a flood. Panning it patiently reveals a few bright flecks among black sand.
A cut bank exposes a shelf of smooth gray clay. It comes away cleanly if dug before the current undercuts the bank again.
Red jasper pebbles gleam beneath shallow water. Sorting them from common river stones takes a careful eye.
A jam of drift logs has built against a bend, leaving several straight trunks caught above the waterline. Freeing them shifts the whole pile.
Willows grow thick along the bank, their shoots long and flexible. Cutting the straight poles cleanly keeps the stand alive.
An abandoned dock slumps into the river, its outer planks waterlogged but its inner boards sound. Salvage must happen before the current tears it loose.
